normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.requirement_title: The app performs key management according to industry best practices.
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-CRYPTO-2 (Description): Even the strongest cryptography would be compromised by poor key management. This control covers the management of cryptographic keys throughout their lifecycle, including key generation, storage and protection.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps that use cryptography must manage cryptographic keys across their full lifecycle (generation, scope, storage, rotation, protection, destruction) according to industry best practices (typically defined in external standards such as NIST SP 800-57 Part 1). Keys must be generated via cryptographically-secure RNGs, scoped to declared purpose, stored in platform secure storage, rotated according to declared policy, protected against side-channel exposure (logs, backups, crash reports), and destroyed via platform-supported mechanisms. The control spans the full key lifecycle, not just key storage.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note: Applies to every app that declares any cryptographic primitive in its security_manifest.crypto_inventory[]. Per Constitution Pillar 3 (Cryptography & Dependencies), key management discipline is universal at Tier 1+. Apps with no cryptography (empty crypto_inventory[]) are formally inapplicable. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 1+ has at least one cryptographic key in scope because storage, transport, and integrity primitives require keys.

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 3 directly covers the key-lifecycle discipline of MASVS-CRYPTO-2 via SEC-PILLAR-3-KEY-MANAGEMENT (primary trace): generation via cryptographically-secure RNGs, declared scope/purpose, platform secure storage, declared rotation period in secrets_inventory[], zeroed-out destruction, prohibition against hardcoding/logging/cross-purpose reuse. Supporting traces extend coverage: SEC-PILLAR-3-SECURE-STORAGE (platform Keychain/Keystore as canonical key storage mechanism), SEC-PILLAR-3-CRYPTO-PRIMITIVES (key-generation primitive selection — Argon2id KDF, X25519/Ed25519 generation, AES key generation backed by platform RNG), and SEC-PILLAR-3-DEPENDENCY-PINNING (key-management library supply-chain discipline). MASVS-CRYPTO-2 is broader than any single Constitution control because it spans implementation correctness across the full lifecycle: RNG usage verification, IV/nonce uniqueness, rotation trigger correctness, and destruction-discipline verification. The implementation-correctness layer is evaluated by file_standards_auditor (per-file key-handling pattern scan) and reaper-crypto + reaper-secrets (adversarial audit of key lifecycle in code). The mapping is partial because no single Constitution control alone captures the full breadth of MASVS-CRYPTO-2.

expected_evidence_patterns: security_manifest.secrets_inventory[] declares every cryptographic key the app holds with classification, scope, retention, and rotation discipline
Key generation calls use cryptographically-secure platform RNGs (SecRandomCopyBytes on iOS, SecureRandom on Android), not application-level RNGs
Long-lived keys declare a rotation_period field in secrets_inventory[]; short-lived session keys declare scope and lifetime
Key storage routes through platform secure storage APIs (Keychain Services / SecKeyCreateRandomKey on iOS; Android Keystore / KeyGenParameterSpec on Android)
Key destruction in generated code uses platform-supported mechanisms (CryptoKit handles automatic zeroing for SymmetricKey; Android keystore-managed keys destroyed via KeyStore.deleteEntry)
Tier 4+: keys held in non-swappable memory pages where W^X policy declared in manifest (SEC-PILLAR-5-WX-MEMORY supporting evidence)
failure_indicators: Hardcoded cryptographic keys in source code, asset bundles, configuration files, or environment variables baked into release binaries
Cryptographic keys logged to application logs, written to crash reports, or included in analytics payloads
Use of non-CSPRNG random number generators (java.util.Random, kotlin.random.Random, Swift's Int.random(in:), Math.random()) for key generation, nonce, IV, or token generation
Cryptographic keys stored in plaintext on filesystem (UserDefaults on iOS, SharedPreferences on Android without Jetpack Security wrapping)
Key reuse across incompatible purposes (e.g., same key used for both encryption and signing without explicit cryptographic separation)
Long-lived keys (>90 days) declared without rotation_period in secrets_inventory[]
IV/nonce reuse in symmetric encryption (same IV used for multiple AES-GCM operations with the same key) — catastrophic confidentiality failure
false_positive_traps: Key handling inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test mocks legitimately use deterministic key material for fixture reproducibility; these are not production violations.

Public keys (verification keys, certificate pinning anchors) embedded in source or bundles are NOT key-material violations — public keys are public by design. The violation is for secret/private key material.

Key derivation intermediate values (transient PBKDF2/Argon2id outputs that immediately feed into a downstream operation) are not 'keys' in the lifecycle sense if they never persist beyond the derivation scope. Persistent derived keys ARE in scope and must be declared in secrets_inventory[].
References to deprecated key-handling patterns in comments, documentation, migration guides, or deprecation warnings are not violations — only their use in security-relevant code paths is a violation

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 3 key-management discipline (SEC-PILLAR-3-KEY-MANAGEMENT) as primary evidence toward MASVS-CRYPTO-2 coverage
leverages Constitution Pillar 3 platform-secure-storage requirement (SEC-PILLAR-3-SECURE-STORAGE) as supporting evidence for key storage
applies Constitution Pillar 3 dependency-pinning discipline (SEC-PILLAR-3-DEPENDENCY-PINNING) to key-management library supply chain
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-CRYPTO-2. MASVS-CRYPTO-2 is mapped to four Constitution v0.5 traces in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-3-KEY-MANAGEMENT (primary), SEC-PILLAR-3-SECURE-STORAGE (supporting), SEC-PILLAR-3-CRYPTO-PRIMITIVES (supporting, key-generation primitives), and SEC-PILLAR-3-DEPENDENCY-PINNING (supporting, supply chain). The lifecycle scope is broad and implementation-correctness verification (RNG usage, IV uniqueness, rotation triggers, destruction discipline) requires auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration. Claims about resistance to side-channel attacks against key material (timing, power, cache attacks) are out of scope for this control unless explicitly declared at Tier 4+. Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
